benchmark_notes.selected_for: Reasoning-type questions for LLM grading benchmark

questions.question_text: Choose True/False (8 points) and give a short explanation (40 points) for each of the following statements.

subparts.question_text: Let Non-P be the class of problems that can be only solved by non-polynomial algorithms, then NonP ∩ NP = ∅.
If we can find a polynomial-time reduction from 3SAT to the language {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} then P = NP.

If P ≠ NP, then any algorithm for solving the 3-color problem must take time at least 3^n on graphs with n vertices. (3-color problem is the problem of deciding whether there is an assignment of one of 3 colors to each vertex of a graph such that no two adjacent vertices receive the same color.)
If someone proves that 3SAT can be reduced to 2SAT in O(n^3) time, then we can solve all NP-complete problems in polynomial time.
If 2SAT can be reduced to 3SAT in O(n^3) time, then we can solve all NP problems in polynomial time.
If there is a problem that is neither in P nor in NP-complete then P ≠ NP.

questions.question_text: Prove that the Knapsack problem (as defined in the lecture) is in NP.
Prove that the problem of finding the longest path between two nodes in a weighted graph is in NP.

A Hamiltonian path in an undirected or directed graph is a path that visits each vertex exactly once. A Tonian path in an undirected or directed graph is a path that visits at least half of the vertices in the graph. Let HAMILTONIAN and TONIAN be the problems of determining whether a graph has a Hamiltonian path and a Tonian path, respectively. Prove that HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN, meaning that any instance of the HAMILTONIAN problem can be reduced in polynomial time to an instance of the TONIAN problem.

Let MATCH be the following problem
- Input: A finite set S of strings, all of the same length n, over the alphabet {0, 1, 2}.
- Output: Determine whether there is a string w ∈ {0, 1}^n such that for every string s ∈ S, the strings s and w have the same symbol in at least one position.

Example 1:
- Input: S = {01220, 21110, 21120, 00211, 11101}
- Output: True, because the string w = 01001 matches the first three strings of S in the second position, and matches the last two strings of S in the last position.

Example 2:
- Input: S = {00, 11, 01, 10}
- Output: False, since we can not find any binary string of length two that matches all strings of S in at least one position.

Prove that MATCH is NP-complete. (Hint: You may assume the following problems from lecture and homework are NP-complete: SAT, 3SAT, SUBSET-SUM, VERTEX-COVER, SET-COVER, CLIQUE, 3COLOR.)
